skill.name: py-fastapi-patterns
skill.description: FastAPI guidance for API design, dependency injection, response models, errors, validation, middleware, OpenAPI, and router organization.
skill.summary: Apply practical FastAPI patterns that keep APIs consistent, typed, documented, and easier for frontend teams to consume.
skill.author: CJHarmath

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and medium findings, but review shows most are false positives from Markdown code fences, inline backticks, HTTP status codes, and FastAPI examples. The only confirmed risk factors are documented diagnostic commands using grep and curl plus hardcoded local or documentation URLs; no prompt injection, credential access, hidden execution, or data exfiltration was found.

low_findings.title: Documented Shell Diagnostics
low_findings.description: The skill includes grep and curl commands for manually checking FastAPI route definitions and a local OpenAPI endpoint. These are not hidden execution paths, but they can read project filenames and contact a local development service if a user runs them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit examples in a Markdown bash block. They are limited to local source search and localhost OpenAPI inspection, with no evidence of exfiltration or remote command retrieval.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Local and Documentation URLs
low_findings.description: The skill references localhost for CORS and OpenAPI examples, and links to FastAPI documentation. These URLs are expected for FastAPI guidance and do not send secrets or contact unknown infrastructure.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible, benign, and contextually tied to local development and public FastAPI documentation. No credential material or arbitrary destination is present.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives
low_findings.description: Many reported external command and weak cryptography findings are Markdown syntax, API examples, status codes, or terms such as OpenAPI schema and hashed_password. They do not represent executable Ruby backticks, weak cryptographic algorithms, or system reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review confirms these locations are educational Markdown and Python snippets. They lack dynamic execution, cryptographic implementation, host probing, or malicious instructions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Manual Command Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The only executable patterns are user-visible diagnostic commands in the Detection Commands section. They should be treated as commands a user may choose to run, not as automatic skill behavior.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are real shell commands, but the surrounding context is instructional and local. This supports a low-risk warning instead of a higher severity finding.

content.value_statement: FastAPI projects can drift into inconsistent routes, weak schemas, and confusing frontend contracts. This skill provides focused patterns for clean endpoints, validation, errors, middleware, and OpenAPI output.
actual_capabilities: Shows dependency injection patterns for sessions, authentication, and active-user checks.
Explains response_model usage to prevent leaking internal model fields.
Provides FastAPI error handling examples with HTTPException and custom handlers.
Documents route ordering rules for specific and parameterized paths.
Covers Pydantic validation, middleware setup, OpenAPI descriptions, and router organization.
Includes diagnostic commands for finding missing response models and route ordering issues.
limitations: It is guidance content, not an automated FastAPI code generator.
Examples use placeholder models and functions that must be adapted to each project.
It does not cover deployment, database migrations, or production observability.
The diagnostic commands assume a local Python project and a running FastAPI server.

use_cases.title: Standardize New API Endpoints
use_cases.description: Use the patterns to create routes with dependency injection, response models, validation, and clear status codes.

use_cases.title: Improve Frontend API Contracts
use_cases.description: Review OpenAPI and response model choices so generated frontend clients receive stable types.

use_cases.title: Review FastAPI Pull Requests
use_cases.description: Check route ordering, error responses, schema descriptions, and middleware choices before merging.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Endpoint
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the py-fastapi-patterns skill to draft a FastAPI endpoint for creating a resource. Include dependencies, request validation, response_model, and correct status codes.

prompt_templates.title: Review Response Models
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the py-fastapi-patterns skill to review these FastAPI routes for missing response_model values, exposed internal fields, and OpenAPI schema problems.

prompt_templates.title: Fix Route and Error Patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the py-fastapi-patterns skill to reorganize these routes and error handlers. Flag route ordering issues, inconsistent errors, and missing validation.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Frontend-Safe API Module
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the py-fastapi-patterns skill to design a FastAPI router module that supports frontend code generation. Include schemas, dependencies, pagination, OpenAPI metadata, and common failure responses.

output_examples.input: Review my user routes for FastAPI pattern issues.
output: A prioritized review of missing response models, route ordering problems, and validation gaps.
Suggested endpoint structure that keeps user data filtered and OpenAPI output accurate.
A route design with a typed paginated response, query parameter limits, and dependency injection.
Notes on schema descriptions and status codes that support frontend client generation.

output_examples.input: Explain why my /users/me route is not reached.
output_examples.output: An explanation of FastAPI route matching order and a corrected route order that places specific paths before generic parameters.

best_practices: Define explicit response models for every route that returns application data.
Place specific routes before parameterized routes to avoid incorrect matches.
Keep OpenAPI descriptions accurate because frontend code generation depends on them.
anti_patterns: Returning ORM or database models directly without filtering fields.
Putting generic parameterized routes before specific routes such as /users/me.
Using broad CORS settings or undocumented schemas in production-facing APIs.

faq.question: Does this skill write FastAPI code automatically?
faq.answer: It provides patterns and examples. The assistant can use them to draft code when you ask for implementation.

faq.question: What FastAPI topics does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers dependencies, response models, errors, route ordering, parameters, validation, middleware, OpenAPI, and router organization.

faq.question: Can it help prevent data leaks?
faq.answer: Yes. It emphasizes response_model usage so internal fields such as password hashes are not returned by accident.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: No. It includes a public documentation link and a localhost curl example, but the skill itself does not require network access.

faq.question: Is it useful for frontend teams?
faq.answer: Yes. It focuses on OpenAPI quality because frontend client generation depends on accurate schemas.

faq.question: What should I adapt before using the examples?
faq.answer: Replace placeholder models, authentication helpers, database sessions, and validation rules with project-specific implementations.
